settings.title: What Happens at Four in the Morning
settings.excerpt: Our head baker Marcus starts long before the lights are on out front, and he likes it that way.
settings.body:
By the time the first customer pushes the door open, our team at Crumbly has already been at it for hours. Marcus, who runs the early shift, clocks in at four.

The first job is the croissants, which were shaped and proofed the night before. Then the breads, then the morning buns, all timed so the cases are full by seven.

The order of the morning

There is a logic to which things go in the oven first, and it is mostly about timing and temperature. Croissants come first because they were shaped the evening before and need to bake while the oven is hottest. The breads follow, since they hold their heat and can wait a few minutes longer. The morning buns go last so they reach the case still warm. Marcus keeps the whole sequence in his head and rarely glances at a written list. After years of it, the timing lives in his hands.

The quiet hours are not only peaceful, they are practical. Doing the delicate work before the front opens means no interruptions and no rush, which is exactly what laminated dough needs.

What gets done before seven

  • Croissants baked first, while the oven is at its hottest.
  • Breads next, then the morning buns timed to land warm.
  • Every case filled and faced before the first customer arrives.

Marcus says the quiet is the best part. Just the radio, the smell of butter, and a list of trays in his head. We are glad someone loves that hour, because most of us do not.
